excerpt: If blocks are produced too quickly, difficulty goes up; too slowly and it goes down. Because the halving schedule is counted in blocks rather than wall-clock time, the difficulty adjustment is what binds the supply curve to calendar time. Without it, the issuance schedule would drift with hashrate.
body:
Source excerpt:
If blocks are produced too quickly, difficulty goes up; too slowly and it goes down. Because the halving schedule is counted in blocks rather than wall-clock time, the difficulty adjustment is what binds the supply curve to calendar time. Without it, the issuance schedule would drift with hashrate.

---

Cited by leaf "The difficulty adjustment":
Every 2,016 blocks — roughly two weeks — the protocol looks at how long the previous 2,016 blocks took to find and retargets the difficulty so that, going forward, blocks should land every ten minutes on average [1]. If the last epoch took less than two weeks, difficulty goes up; if it took more, it goes down. The clamp is hard-coded to ±4× per adjustment so a sudden hashrate swing can't run away in a single epoch.

The mechanism is what makes Bitcoin's issuance schedule predictable. Block subsidy halves every 210,000 blocks, which is exactly 100 epochs, so the supply curve is timed to wall-clock years only because the difficulty adjustment keeps blocks landing at the target rate regardless of how much hashrate joins or leaves [2]. When China kicked out miners in mid-2021 and roughly half the hashrate went offline overnight, blocks slowed to ~14 minutes for two weeks, then the adjustment dropped difficulty by 28% and the chain returned to ten minutes — no manual intervention, no developer call, no governance vote.

The edge cases are worth knowing. The retarget reads timestamps from block headers, which miners can lie about within bounds set by other rules (median-of-eleven past blocks, two hours into the future); this is exploited by a known-but-impractical "timewarp" attack that has never been used [3]. There is also a long-running off-by-one in the exact window the calculation reads (2,015 blocks not 2,016) — a known quirk preserved for backward compatibility, not a bug worth fixing.
